subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 13! In this unit you will learn how mathematicians compare quantities and how they scale things up and down while keeping them in exactly the same relationship. This is the world of ratio and proportion, and once you can see it, you will notice it everywhere. When Amina makes a necklace at the Hargeisa market with blue and white beads, when Hassan mixes paint for a wall, when Khadija doubles a bariis recipe to feed extra guests at iftar, or when a mapmaker shrinks the whole distance from Mogadishu to Kismaayo onto a single page, they are all using ratio and proportion.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Write a ratio using the colon notation (for example 3:2) and read it correctly.
Describe a ratio with ‘for every’ language and a proportion with ‘out of’ language.
Simplify a ratio to its lowest terms by dividing by the highest common factor.
Recognise and generate equivalent ratios using ratio tables.
Share a total quantity in a given ratio, including three-part ratios.
Solve direct proportion problems using the unit (one-part) method.
Use ratio and proportion to scale recipes, maps and models up or down.
Tell the difference between a ratio (part to part) and a proportion (part to whole), and convert proportions to fractions, decimals and percentages.

concepts.title: What a Ratio Is and How We Write It
concepts.explanation: A ratio compares two or more quantities by showing how many of one thing there are for each amount of another. A good way to hear a ratio is to ask: “For every ___ of this, how many of that do we have?” Ratios are all about the phrase ‘for every’. Let us start with a real example. Amina is threading a necklace to sell at the market. She uses 6 blue beads and 2 white beads. The ratio of blue beads to white beads is written 6:2, using the colon symbol, and we read it aloud as “six to two.” The colon is like the word…
concepts.example: There are two ideas about ratios you must never forget. First, order matters. The ratio 3:1 is not the same as 1:3. If we say the ratio of blue beads to white beads is 3:1, that means there are more blue beads. If we…

concepts.title: Simplifying Ratios to Lowest Terms
concepts.explanation: Ratios behave much like fractions. Just as the fraction 6/8 can be written more simply as 3/4, the ratio 6:2 can be written more simply as 3:1. Simplifying a ratio means dividing every term by the same number until you cannot divide any further. The result is called the ratio in its lowest terms. Look again at Amina’s necklace: the ratio of blue to white beads is 6:2. Both terms can be divided by 2. Doing so gives 6 ÷ 2 = 3 and 2 ÷ 2 = 1, so 6:2 = 3:1. This tells us something beautifully simple: “for every 3 blue…
concepts.example: To simplify quickly, look for the highest common factor of the terms – the largest number that divides them all exactly. Consider the ratio 12:8. The number 4 divides both 12 and 8, and it is the largest number that…

concepts.title: Equivalent Ratios and Ratio Tables
concepts.explanation: Equivalent ratios are different-looking ratios that describe exactly the same relationship, in the same way that 1/2, 2/4 and 3/6 are equivalent fractions. For example, 2:3, 4:6, 6:9 and 8:12 are all equivalent. Each one says “for every 2 of the first thing there are 3 of the second thing” – they are just written at different sizes. To make an equivalent ratio, you multiply or divide both terms by the same number. Starting with 2:3 and multiplying both terms by 2 gives 4:6. Multiplying both terms by 3 gives 6:9.…
concepts.example: Here is the single most important warning in this whole unit: you must do the same operation to both terms. A common mistake is to add the same number to each term – that does not keep a ratio equivalent. For instance,…

concepts.title: Sharing a Quantity in a Ratio
concepts.explanation: One of the most useful things a ratio can do is help you share a total fairly. Suppose Omar and Yusuf sell mangoes together at the market and agree to split the money in the ratio 3:2, because Omar did more of the work. At the end of the day they have earned 60 shillings. How much should each boy receive? This is a sharing in a ratio problem, and there is a reliable four-step method. Add the parts of the ratio to find the total number of parts. For 3:2, the total is 3 + 2 = 5 parts.
concepts.example: Divide the total quantity by the number of parts to find the value of one part. Here, 60 ÷ 5 = 12 shillings per part.

concepts.title: Direct Proportion and the Unit Method
concepts.explanation: Two quantities are in direct proportion when they grow and shrink together at the same rate. If you double one, the other doubles too. If you halve one, the other halves. Direct proportion is the mathematics behind recipes, shopping, and fair pricing, and the most powerful tool for solving it is the unit method – find the value of one, then multiply. Here is a shopping example. In the market, 3 pencils cost 6 shillings. How much do 9 pencils cost? Using the unit method, first find the cost of one pencil: 6 ÷ 3 = 2…
concepts.example: Cooking is full of direct proportion. Khadija has a rice dish that serves 4 people and uses 2 cups of rice and 3 cups of water. She needs to serve 12 people at an Eid gathering. Because 12 ÷ 4 = 3, she is making three…

concepts.title: Scaling with Maps and Models
concepts.explanation: A scale is a special ratio that compares the size of a drawing or model with the size of the real thing. Scales let a whole city fit on a page and let an architect hold a building in the palm of a hand. A scale is usually written as a ratio such as 1:100, which means “1 unit on the model stands for 100 of the same units in real life.” Think about a model of a building where the scale is 1:100. This tells us that 1 cm on the model represents 100 cm – that is 1 metre – in reality. So a door that measures 3 cm on the…
concepts.example: Maps use scale in exactly the same way. Suppose a map has a scale of 1:25,000, and two masjids are 6 cm apart on the map. To find the real distance, multiply: 6 × 25,000 = 150,000 cm. Now convert to a friendlier unit:…

methods.title: How to simplify a ratio
methods.example: In a class of 24 students there are 15 girls and 9 boys. Write the ratio of girls to boys in its simplest form.
steps: Find the highest common factor of the terms.
Divide every term by that factor.
Check that no number except 1 divides all the terms.

methods.title: How to share a quantity in a ratio
methods.example: The ratio of cats to dogs in a shelter is 4:7. If there are 16 cats, how many dogs are there?
steps: Add the parts of the ratio to find the total number of parts.
Divide the total quantity by the number of parts to find one part.
Multiply each share by the value of one part.
Check by adding all the shares back to the original total.

methods.title: How to solve a direct proportion (unit method)
methods.example: Two numbers are in the ratio 2:5. If their sum is 35, what are the two numbers?
steps: Find the value of one item by dividing.
Multiply by how many items you want.
Check the two quantities grew at the same rate.

methods.title: How to use a map or model scale
methods.example: A recipe uses flour and sugar in the ratio 3:2. If you use 9 cups of flour, how much sugar do you need?
steps: Model to real life: multiply by the scale.
Real life to model: divide by the scale.
Convert units carefully: 100 cm = 1 m, 1,000 m = 1 km.

methods.title: Use the unit method
methods.example: If 3 pencils cost 2 shillings, how much do 12 pencils cost?
steps: Solution: Find the cost of one pencil first: 2 ÷ 3 shillings each.
Then multiply by 12: (2 ÷ 3) × 12 = 2 × 12 ÷ 3 = 24 ÷ 3 = 8 shillings.
The unit method handled the tricky division for us.

methods.title: Turn a proportion into a percentage
methods.example: Express 7 out of 20 as a percentage.
steps: Solution: The proportion is the fraction 7/20.
Divide to make a decimal: 7 ÷ 20 = 0.35.
Multiply by 100 to make a percentage: 0.35 × 100 = 35%.
So 7 out of 20 is the same as 35%.

workedExamples.title: Write and simplify a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: In a class of 24 students there are 15 girls and 9 boys. Write the ratio of girls to boys in its simplest form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The ratio of girls to boys is 15:9. The highest common factor of 15 and 9 is 3. Dividing both terms by 3 gives 15 ÷ 3 = 5 and 9 ÷ 3 = 3, so the simplest form is 5:3. This means for every 5 girls there are 3 boys.

workedExamples.title: Find a missing value in a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: The ratio of cats to dogs in a shelter is 4:7. If there are 16 cats, how many dogs are there?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The number of cats went from 4 up to 16, which is ×4 (because 16 ÷ 4 = 4). To keep the ratio equivalent, multiply the dogs by the same 4: 7 × 4 = 28 dogs. Check: 16:28 simplifies by dividing by 4 back to 4:7, which matches.

workedExamples.title: Share a quantity in a ratio
workedExamples.prompt: Two numbers are in the ratio 2:5. If their sum is 35, what are the two numbers?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the parts: 2 + 5 = 7 parts. Find one part: 35 ÷ 7 = 5. Multiply: the first number is 2 × 5 = 10, and the second is 5 × 5 = 25. Check: 10 + 25 = 35, and 10:25 simplifies to 2:5. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Scale a recipe (direct proportion)
workedExamples.prompt: A recipe uses flour and sugar in the ratio 3:2. If you use 9 cups of flour, how much sugar do you need?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The flour went from 3 up to 9, which is ×3. Keep the ratio by multiplying the sugar by 3 as well: 2 × 3 = 6 cups of sugar. The ratio stays 9:6, which simplifies back to 3:2.

workedExamples.title: Use the unit method
workedExamples.prompt: If 3 pencils cost 2 shillings, how much do 12 pencils cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the cost of one pencil first: 2 ÷ 3 shillings each. Then multiply by 12: (2 ÷ 3) × 12 = 2 × 12 ÷ 3 = 24 ÷ 3 = 8 shillings. The unit method handled the tricky division for us.

workedExamples.title: Turn a proportion into a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: Express 7 out of 20 as a percentage.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The proportion is the fraction 7/20. Divide to make a decimal: 7 ÷ 20 = 0.35. Multiply by 100 to make a percentage: 0.35 × 100 = 35%. So 7 out of 20 is the same as 35%.

workedExamples.title: A map-scale problem
workedExamples.prompt: On a map with scale 1:25,000, two towns are 6 cm apart. What is the real distance in kilometres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply the map distance by the scale: 6 × 25,000 = 150,000 cm. Convert: 150,000 cm = 1,500 m = 1.5 km. Remember to keep dividing by 100 for metres and by 1,000 for kilometres.

workedExamples.title: Share a three-part ratio
workedExamples.prompt: Three numbers are in the ratio 2:3:5 and their sum is 60. Find all three numbers.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the parts: 2 + 3 + 5 = 10 parts. Find one part: 60 ÷ 10 = 6. Multiply each: 2 × 6 = 12, 3 × 6 = 18, 5 × 6 = 30. Check: 12 + 18 + 30 = 60. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Express each proportion as a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: Express each proportion as a percentage: a) 3 out of 10 b) 7 out of 20 c) 9 out of 25.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: If 3 pencils cost 2 shillings, how much do 12 pencils cost? Use the unit method.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A paint mix uses red and yellow in the ratio 2:5. If you use 6 litres of red, how much yellow do you need?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Three numbers are in the ratio 2:3:5 and their sum is 60. Find all three numbers.

activities.title: Recipe Master Chef
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, measuring cups (optional), and a simple recipe.
steps: Take Khadija’s rice recipe that serves 4 people: 2 cups of rice and 3 cups of water.
Scale it to serve 8 people by multiplying every ingredient by 2. Write down the new amounts.
Now scale it to serve 12 people. What did you multiply by this time?
Scale it back down to serve just 2 people. What is the ratio of rice to water each time?
You should find the ratio of rice to water stays 2:3 no matter how many people you serve. That constant ratio is what keeps the dish tasting right.

activities.title: Bead Necklace Ratios
activities.materials: beads, buttons, or small stones in two colours, and some string.
steps: Make a pattern with blue and white beads in the ratio 3:1 (three blue, then one white, repeating).
After making the pattern five times, count your beads. What is the ratio now? Simplify it.
Work out the proportion of beads that are blue as a fraction and a percentage.
Try a new ratio, such as 2:3, and describe your necklace using ‘for every’ language.

activities.title: Fair Share Investigation
activities.materials: counters, dried beans, or small stones to act as ‘shillings’.
steps: Count out 40 ‘shillings’. Share them between two people in the ratio 3:5 using the four-step method.
Check your shares add back to 40. Did each person get a whole number of shillings?
Now share 24 dates between three cousins in the ratio 3:2:1. How many parts are there in total?
Invent your own sharing problem for a family member and let them solve it.

activities.title: Map and Scale Adventure
activities.materials: a ruler, paper, and a printed or drawn map with a scale.
steps: Draw a simple map of your neighbourhood where 1 cm stands for 100 m (a scale of 1:10,000).
Mark your home, the masjid, and the market. Measure the distances in centimetres.
Multiply each distance by the scale to find the real distance in metres, then in kilometres.
Challenge: if you walk at about 5 km per hour, how long would each journey take?

activities.title: Paint and Colour Mixing (Math Game)
activities.materials: two players, and coloured counters or pencils.
steps: Player 1 names a colour ratio, such as red to yellow = 2:5.
Player 2 must lay out counters to match, then simplify or scale the ratio on request.
Score a point for each correct ratio and each correct simplification.
Swap roles and play to ten points. Keep a ratio table to check each other’s work.

activities.title: Shopping Value Detective (Family Task)
activities.materials: two similar products with different sizes and prices (or make some up).
steps: Find two packets of the same food in different sizes, for example 500 g for 4 shillings and 750 g for 6 shillings.
Work out the price of the same amount for each, such as the price per 100 g or per kilogram.
Decide which packet gives better value for money, and explain your reasoning to your family.
Do this for three more pairs of products and record which was better value each time.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand ratio and proportion best? What was the trickiest part? Where in your daily life do you now notice ratios – in cooking, shopping, sharing, or maps? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Ratio Rule
rules.text: A ratio compares part to part, written with a colon: 3:2 is read three to two. Order matters: 3:2 is not the same as 2:3. Simplify by dividing every term by the same number.

rules.title: Equivalent Ratio Rule
rules.text: Multiply or divide both terms by the same number to keep a ratio equivalent. Never add or subtract: 2:3 is not equivalent to 3:4.

rules.title: Proportion Rule
rules.text: A proportion compares a part to the whole. It can be written as a fraction, a decimal, or a percentage. 6 out of 8 = 6/8 = 3/4 = 0.75 = 75%.

terms: A part-to-part comparison, written with a colon
One of the numbers in a ratio
Divide all terms by the same number to lowest terms
Different ratios showing the same relationship
Quantities that grow and shrink at the same rate
The ratio of a model or map to the real thing
All the parts of a ratio added together
commonMistakes: Write terms in the order named
Divide both terms by 4 to get 2:3
Multiply both terms by the same number
Ask: part to part, or part to whole?

questions.question: What does “Ratio” mean?
options: A part-to-part comparison, written with a colon
One of the numbers in a ratio
Divide all terms by the same number to lowest terms
Different ratios showing the same relationship

selfAssessment: I can write a ratio using the colon notation (for example 3:2) and read it correctly.
I can describe a ratio with ‘for every’ language and a proportion with ‘out of’ language.
I can simplify a ratio to its lowest terms by dividing by the highest common factor.
I can recognise and generate equivalent ratios using ratio tables.
I can share a total quantity in a given ratio, including three-part ratios.
I can solve direct proportion problems using the unit (one-part) method.
I can use ratio and proportion to scale recipes, maps and models up or down.
I can tell the difference between a ratio (part to part) and a proportion (part to whole), and convert proportions to fractions, decimals and percentages.
